student_C.reasoning: The submission is 121 hours late, which crosses the explicit cutoff of 5 days (120 hours) or more. Therefore the consistent outcome is a zero, regardless of how the 10-points-per-24-hours rule would apply before that cutoff.

results.stem: A student claims: “Once you do feature extraction, classification is always 100% certain, so probabilities are unnecessary.” Which response is best supported by the slides, integrating the PR definition/examples with the PR system diagram and typical classification output?

student_A.memory_reflection: I mostly just remember pattern recognition and some example with apples and pears. I don’t remember the diagram details or what the output looked like.

option_analysis.A: Sounds very absolute (100% certainty) and I don’t really remember anything about regression tasks.
option_analysis.B: Talks about fixed rules; I guess that could be a thing but it sounds kind of rigid and I don’t remember that.
option_analysis.C: Mentions losing information/features and says output is 'The probability of each class!' That phrase sounds like something that could literally be on a slide/diagram.
option_analysis.D: Has '01→apple' which matches the apple memory, but the 'only used in unsupervised' part sounds random to me.

student_A.reasoning: Since I don’t really know, I’m picking the option that contains a very slide-like quoted phrase ('The probability of each class!') and also it kind of fits with the vague idea that apples/pears might be uncertain sometimes.

student_B.memory_reflection: I remember the apple vs pear example where missing information like color means you can’t be 100% certain even if you extract features. I also remember a PR system diagram: raw data → features → classifier, and the output was described like probabilities, with 0 and 1 related to probability. Also PR and ML were said to be similar and not like fixed rule-based algorithms.

option_analysis.A: This contradicts the memory that you can’t be 100% certain if information is missing. Also I don’t remember anything about regression tasks here.
option_analysis.B: This sounds like rule-based algorithms, but the slides said PR/ML are unlike fixed human-made rules. So this doesn’t match.
option_analysis.C: This matches both parts I remember: losing info/features prevents 100% certainty, and the classifier output is usually probabilities of each class.
option_analysis.D: I don’t remember anything about unsupervised vs supervised from the trace. Also the output being probabilities seems to contradict 'only hard label'.

student_B.reasoning: From the apple/pear example, even with feature extraction you might miss key info (like color), so you can’t be fully certain. And the system diagram emphasized classifier output as probabilities (like 'probability of each class', with 0/1 linked to probability). So the best-supported response is that probabilities are still central rather than unnecessary.

student_C.memory_reflection: From the lecture: there was an apple-vs-pear image example where, if the item is correctly extracted from the right part of the image, it sounded like you could classify with 100% certainty. But right after that, the slide questioned whether 100% certainty is always possible. In the follow-up example, some information/features were lost (explicitly color), so the decision likely cannot be made with 100% certainty and we should determine what the picture is with uncertainty-aware methods. The PR system diagram showed raw data x -> preprocessing/feature extraction/dimension reduction -> classification/regression -> output y, and it asked what 0 and 1 represent, answering that they represent probability (so the classifier output is probabilistic confidence). Another slide said typical classification output is: 'The probability of each class!'

option_analysis.A: Contradicts the slide that questions 100% certainty and the example where losing color prevents certainty. Also the memory says classification outputs probabilities, not only regression.
option_analysis.B: Doesn't match the PR/ML framing: ML/PR is data-centric and outputs probabilities; relying on fixed predetermined rules is described as non-learning/human-centric, not the main point here.
option_analysis.C: Matches both parts: (1) information loss in feature extraction can prevent 100% certainty, and (2) the system diagram/ML framework explicitly says outputs are probabilities of each class.

student_C.memory_reflection: From the slides: independence means P(A,B)=P(A)P(B). Mutually exclusive means they cannot happen together, so P(A,B)=0. Also P(A ∨ B)=P(A)+P(B)−P(A,B), and conditional probability is P(B|A)=P(A,B)/P(A).

option_analysis.A: If mutually exclusive, P(A,B)=0, so it cannot generally equal P(A)+P(B) unless P(A)=P(B)=0. Not a must-true statement.
option_analysis.B: P(A ∨ B)=P(A)+P(B) for mutually exclusive events (since P(A,B)=0). Independence gives P(A)P(B)=P(A,B)=0, but that doesn't force P(A ∨ B) to equal P(A)P(B) except in special cases. So not must-true.
option_analysis.C: From conditional probability: P(B|A)=P(A,B)/P(A). With mutual exclusivity, P(A,B)=0 so P(B|A)=0/P(A)=0 if P(A)>0. option_analysis.D: Independence gives P(A,B)=P(A)P(B). Mutual exclusivity gives P(A,B)=0. So P(A)P(B)=0, which implies at least one of P(A) or P(B) is 0. This must be true.

Discrete RVs take countably many values (example: number of students) and their distribution is given by a probability mass function (pmf) p(x) = P(X = x), with the normalization sum_x p(x) = 1.

Continuous RVs take infinitely many real values (example: time to complete a task) and are described via the cumulative distribution function P(x) ≜ P(X ≤ x) and a probability density function (pdf) p(x) = dP(x)/dx.
For a continuous RV, interval probabilities are computed by P(a < X ≤ b) = ∫_a^b p(x) dx = P(b) − P(a).

student_C.memory_reflection: From the slide, Bayes’ rule was p(H=h|Y=y) = p(H=h)p(Y=y|H=h)/p(Y=y), with posterior ∝ prior × likelihood and p(Y=y) (marginal likelihood) doing the normalization. In the weather example: H=1 means it actually rains, Y=1 means forecast rain. Prior P(H)=5/365=0.0137. Likelihoods: P(Y=1|H=1)=0.9 and P(Y=1|H=0)=0.1. The computed posterior after a rain forecast was P(H=1|Y=1)=0.0137·0.9 / (0.9·0.0137 + 0.1·(1−0.0137)) = 0.111.

option_analysis.D: Incorrect: P(Y|H)=0.9 is not small (it is large). The posterior is low mainly because the prior is tiny and normalization includes the false-alarm term P(Y|¬H)=0.1.

student_C.reasoning: Using posterior ∝ prior × likelihood, the prior P(H)=0.0137 is so low that multiplying by 0.9 still gives a small unnormalized value, and after dividing by p(Y)=0.9·0.0137 + 0.1·(1−0.0137), the posterior becomes 0.111. The likelihood terms were P(Y|H)=0.9 (forecasts rain when it rains) and P(Y|¬H)=0.1 (forecasts rain when it doesn’t rain), and with a rain forecast the posterior was P(H|Y)=0.0137·0.9 / (0.9·0.0137 + 0.1·(1−0.0137)) = 0.111.

There was also a slide about Gaussian/normal, but I don’t recall any details.

I recall the formula had a combinatorial term (N choose s) times something like theta to a power and (1-theta) to another power, but I might mix up which exponent goes where.
Categorical was the version of Bernoulli for more than two outcomes, with one probability parameter per class, and they mentioned one-hot encoding.
Multinomial was like repeating categorical N times and counting how many times each class appears; I remember it reduces to categorical when N=1 and to binomial when there are 2 classes.
Gaussian/normal was for real-valued variables with mean mu and variance sigma-squared, and the pdf is the bell curve with an exponential.
student_C: If we observe N Bernoulli trials y_n ~ Ber(theta) for n=1..N and define s as the number of successes/heads, s := sum_{n=1}^N I(y_n = 1), then s follows a Binomial distribution.
The binomial pmf is Bin(s | N, theta) = (N choose s) theta^s (1-theta)^(N-s), where (N choose s) = N! / ((N-s)! s!).
Bernoulli is a special case of binomial (essentially N=1), and binomial models counts of successes in N independent Bernoulli trials.
Bernoulli/binomial only handle two outcomes, so we generalize to C>2 outcomes with the categorical distribution: Cat(y | theta) = product_{c=1}^C theta_c^{I(y=c)}, so p(y=c | theta)=theta_c.
Using one-hot encoding y (vector) with C elements, Cat(y | theta) is written as product_{c=1}^C theta_c^{y_c}, e.g., for C=3 the one-hots are (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1).

Repeating categorical trials N times and counting occurrences y_c = sum_{n=1}^N I(y_n=c) gives a count vector y (not one-hot) that follows a multinomial distribution with pmf proportional to (N! / (y_1! ... y_C!)) * product_{c=1}^C theta_c^{y_c}, with sum_c y_c = N; multinomial reduces to categorical when N=1 and to binomial when C=2.

For the univariate Gaussian (normal) on real-valued x, x ~ N(mu, sigma^2), the cdf is Phi(x; mu, sigma) = ∫_{-∞}^x N(z | mu, sigma^2) dz = 1/2 (1 + erf(z/√2)) with z=(x-mu)/sigma, and the pdf is N(x | mu, sigma^2) = (1/√(2πσ^2)) exp(-(x-mu)^2/(2σ^2)); E[X]=mu, Var[X]=sigma^2, and E[X^2]=mu^2+sigma^2.

The multinomial pmf is p(y⃗ | N, θ) = (N! / (y_1!…y_C!)) ∏_{c=1}^C θ_c^{y_c}, where θ_c is the probability of category c and the factorial term is the multinomial coefficient counting allocations of N items into subset sizes y_c.
Special cases: if N=1, multinomial becomes categorical; if C=2, multinomial becomes the binomial distribution.

student_C: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) chooses parameters by maximizing the likelihood of the observed dataset: theta_hat = argmax_theta p(D|theta).
Under the iid assumption, the dataset likelihood factorizes as p(D|theta) = product_{n=1}^N p(y_n | x_n, theta), and the log-likelihood is l(theta) = log p(D|theta).
Since most algorithms are posed as minimization, they define the conditional negative log-likelihood (NLL): NLL(theta) = -log p(D|theta) = -sum_{n=1}^N log p(y_n | x_n, theta).
Thus MLE can be written equivalently as theta_hat = argmin_theta L(theta) = argmin_theta NLL(theta).
Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) generalizes MLE by replacing the per-sample log-loss term -log p(y_n|x_n,theta) with an arbitrary loss l(y_n, theta; x_n).
The ERM objective is L(theta) = sum_{n=1}^N l(y_n, theta; x_n), interpreted as minimizing the expected loss under the empirical (sampled) data distribution.

student_C: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) picks parameters θ that maximize the likelihood of the training dataset: θ_hat_MLE = argmax_θ p(D|θ).
Under the iid assumption, the dataset likelihood factorizes as p(D|θ) = ∏_{n=1}^N p(y_n | x_n, θ), and the log-likelihood is ℓ(θ) ≡ log p(D|θ).

To match standard minimization-based optimizers, they define the (conditional) negative log-likelihood: NLL(θ) ≡ -log p(D|θ) = -∑_{n=1}^N log p(y_n|x_n,θ), so maximizing likelihood is equivalent to minimizing NLL.
They then frame this as an objective θ_hat = argmin_θ L(θ) = argmin_θ NLL(θ).
Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) generalizes MLE by replacing the specific log-loss term ℓ_n(θ) = -log p(y_n|x_n,θ) with a general per-sample loss ℓ(y_n,θ; x_n).
In ERM the empirical risk is L(θ) = ∑_{n=1}^N ℓ(y_n,θ; x_n), interpreted as an expected loss under the empirical (sampled) distribution.
For classification they gave the 0-1 loss: ℓ_{01}(y_n,θ; x_n)=0 if y_n = f(x_n;θ) and 1 otherwise, so ∑_{n=1}^N ℓ_{01}(y_n,θ; x_n) is the empirical misclassification rate on the training set.

student_C: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) chooses parameters to maximize the likelihood of the observed dataset: \(\hat{\theta}_{\text{mle}} = \arg\max_{\theta} p(\mathcal{D}\mid \theta)\).

Under the iid assumption for samples \((x_n,y_n)\), the conditional likelihood factorizes: \(p(\mathcal{D}\mid\theta)=\prod_{n=1}^N p(y_n\mid x_n,\theta)\), and the log-likelihood is \(\ell(\theta)=\log p(\mathcal{D}\mid\theta)\).

Because many optimization methods are framed as minimization, we define the (conditional) negative log-likelihood: \(\text{NLL}(\theta) = -\log p(\mathcal{D}\mid\theta)= -\sum_{n=1}^N \log p(y_n\mid x_n,\theta)\), so maximizing likelihood is equivalent to minimizing NLL: \(\hat{\theta}=\arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta)=\arg\min_{\theta}\text{NLL}(\theta)\).
For the Bernoulli coin-toss model with \(Y\in\{0,1\}\) and \(\theta=p(Y=1)\), the MLE ends up as the empirical fraction of heads (sample mean of the \(y_n\)’s), which matches intuition.
For ERM in classification, an intuitive loss is the 0-1 loss: \(\ell_{01}(y_n,\theta;x_n)=0\) if \(y_n=f(x_n;\theta)\) and \(1\) otherwise.
The empirical risk is the sum over the training set, \(\mathcal{L}(\theta)=\sum_{n=1}^N \ell_{01}(y_n,\theta;x_n)\), i.e., the empirical misclassification count/rate on the training data.
